Evidence: Some participants feared that the chlorine used by ambulance workers was toxic and potentially lethal. Participants’ fears may have resulted from real observations.
There was a reported case of a nurse experiencing respiratory distress and brief loss of consciousness after inhalation of highly concentrated chlorine gas at an Ebola treatment unit in Sierra Leone.

Participants reported they would ensure that a fever was due to Ebola, rather than another illness, before seeking care. In the interim they would try to self-medicate or visit local trusted health care facilities.
292.Rationale: The passage indicates that fear of chlorine spray in ambulances was a significant concern for participants. This fear, combined with the real observation of a nurse experiencing adverse effects from chlorine, likely contributed to delayed care-seeking. The text also notes that participants would delay seeking care until they were certain of their diagnosis, which could be exacerbated by fears of the chlorine spray. This aligns with the option that fear of chlorine spray contributed to delayed care-seeking despite high intentions to use the response system.

292.Option: Lack of access to transportation to health facilities resulted in delayed hospitalization despite high intentions to seek care
Evidence: The response system included ambulance workers dressed in personal protective equipment (PPE) who arrived to transport suspected cases to holding centers.

Participants were more willing to call 117 if they observed symptoms in another person. However, about one-third of participants stated they were afraid of community members’ anger if they were to call.
292.Rationale: The passage does not mention a lack of access to transportation as a factor contributing to delayed hospitalization. Instead, it describes the presence of ambulance services as part of the response system. The delay in care-seeking is attributed to fears related to the response system (e.g., chlorine spray, fear of not returning) and misunderstandings about the disease and testing process, not a lack of transportation.

293.Option: Neutralizing antibody titers against the homologous virus are not predictive of protection against heterologous challenge, as the animals were protected despite low cross-reactive titers
Evidence: All three animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).

There was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV (Exact unconditional test) in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).

However, we did observe an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite the lack of detectable viremia in the animals (Fig 4D) suggesting that low level virus replication did occur, indicating protection from disease rather than sterilizing immunity.
293.Rationale: The passage states that animals had nAb titers ≥20 after primary infection and these titers were associated with protection against heterologous challenge. The study also notes that nAb titers increased after re-challenge despite no detectable viremia, suggesting that the antibodies were present and likely contributed to protection. The claim that animals were protected despite low cross-reactive titers is contradicted by the evidence showing detectable and significant nAb titers.

293.Option: Protection from heterologous viremia is mediated by T cell responses rather than neutralizing antibodies, as evidenced by the absence of detectable viremia despite low nAb titers
Evidence: All three animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).

There was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV (Exact unconditional test) in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).

However, we did observe an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite the lack of detectable viremia in the animals (Fig 4D) suggesting that low level virus replication did occur, indicating protection from disease rather than sterilizing immunity.
293.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that animals had detectable nAb titers (≥20) after primary infection and that these titers were associated with protection against heterologous challenge. The claim that protection is mediated by T cell responses rather than nAbs is not supported by the evidence, which emphasizes the role of nAbs in protection. The study also notes that nAb titers increased after re-challenge, further supporting their role in protection.

293.Option: The presence of neutralizing antibodies at any detectable level is sufficient to prevent viremia in heterologous Zika virus challenge, regardless of titer or cross-reactivity
Evidence: All three animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).

There was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV (Exact unconditional test) in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).

However, we did observe an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite the lack of detectable viremia in the animals (Fig 4D) suggesting that low level virus replication did occur, indicating protection from disease rather than sterilizing immunity.
293.Rationale: The passage states that animals had nAb titers ≥20 after primary infection and these titers were associated with protection against heterologous challenge. However, the study also notes that protection was not complete (as indicated by the increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite no detectable viremia), suggesting that the presence of any detectable level of nAbs is not sufficient to prevent viremia. The claim that nAbs at any detectable level are sufficient is not supported by the evidence, which shows that higher titers were associated with better protection.

293.Option: The lack of detectable viremia after heterologous challenge indicates sterilizing immunity, which is likely due to the rapid induction of innate immune responses rather than neutralizing antibodies
Evidence: However, we did observe an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite the lack of detectable viremia in the animals (Fig 4D) suggesting that low level virus replication did occur, indicating protection from disease rather than sterilizing immunity.
293.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the lack of detectable viremia after heterologous challenge does not indicate sterilizing immunity, as low-level virus replication did occur. The claim that sterilizing immunity is due to innate immune responses rather than nAbs is not supported by the evidence, which emphasizes the role of nAbs in protection.

293.Option: High and cross-reactive neutralizing antibody titers are strongly associated with protection from detectable viremia during heterologous Zika virus challenge
Evidence: All three animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).

There was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV (Exact unconditional test) in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).

The peak magnitude of proliferation was slightly lower, on average, than observed following ZIKV-FP infection but the small number of macaques in the study makes it impossible to quantify the significance of this observation.
Animals described here had nAb titers that far surpassed the 1:10 threshold (Fig 4D).
293.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that animals with high nAb titers (≥20) after primary infection with African ZIKV were protected against heterologous challenge with Asian ZIKV. The study also notes that these titers were significantly higher than the 1:10 threshold considered protective in other flaviviruses. The evidence supports the conclusion that high and cross-reactive nAb titers are strongly associated with protection from detectable viremia during heterologous challenge.

294.Option: The association remained significant even after adjusting for infant preterm birth and low birth weight, which are known mediators of the relationship between maternal infection and neurodevelopment
Evidence: Infant characteristics (preterm births, low birth weight, and weight-for-age at MSEL assessment) were hypothesized to be mediators in the association between prenatal helminth infection and infant cognitive function, we adjusted for these variables in a sensitivity analyses.

After adjusting for gravidity, maternal education, family possession, child sex and HOME score, helminth infection at first ANC visit was negatively associated with infant gross motor function (p = 0.028).

We observed that mothers who were infected with hookworms during the first ANC visit had children who scored less in the gross motor scale, -4.9 (95% CI: -8.6 to-1.1), compared to those whose mothers were never infected with hookworms at first ANC visit.
294.Rationale: Adjusting for mediators like preterm birth and low birth weight actually weakens the case against confounding by anemia. Mediators are part of the causal pathway, so adjusting for them removes part of the true effect. The fact that the association remained after adjusting for these mediators suggests the effect is not fully explained by them, but this does not rule out anemia as a confounder. In fact, if anemia were a confounder, adjusting for mediators would not address it.

294.Option: The study used a validated neurodevelopmental assessment tool (MSEL) administered by trained research nurses, reducing measurement error and increasing confidence in the observed association
Evidence: The MSEL which comprises of five scales, gross motor, fine motor, visual reception, receptive language and expressive language, was adapted to this setting prior to its use in this study.
Detailed quality assurance and reliability of assessment have already been published.
Five assessors were trained by MJB and FBL in the field and retrained three times.
294.Rationale: While using a validated tool and trained assessors reduces measurement error, this does not directly address the issue of confounding by anemia. Measurement error in the outcome would affect the precision of the estimate but not the validity of the association in terms of confounding. The question specifically asks about confounding by anemia, not about the reliability of the outcome measurement.

294.Option: The study adjusted for malaria co-infection during pregnancy, which is a known risk factor for anemia and poor neurodevelopment, thereby reducing the likelihood of confounding by iron deficiency
Evidence: Maternal malarial infection was not statistically significantly associated with ELC and gross motor scores (see Table 3).
Malaria has also been assessed several times during pregnancy allowing for the study of the impact of malaria-helminth co-infection on child development.
Despite low power due to the low prevalence of co-infection, our results do not suggest a higher impact on child development of helminths when associated temporally with malaria.
294.Rationale: Adjusting for malaria co-infection is directly relevant to ruling out confounding by anemia. Malaria is a major cause of anemia in pregnant women in this region. By adjusting for malaria, the study accounts for a key potential confounder that could independently contribute to both anemia and poor neurodevelopment. This adjustment strengthens the argument that the observed association is not due to anemia but rather to the helminth infection itself.

294.Option: The association persisted after adjusting for multiple socioeconomic and maternal health confounders, suggesting it is not fully explained by unmeasured anemia
Evidence: Maternal education, occupation, family possession, RPM and HOME scores and infant weight-for-age were associated with ELC and gross motor scores.
We conducted a multiple linear regression adjusting for covariates whose p-values were less than 0.20 in the univariate analysis.

The difference in mean ELC scores between children whose mothers were infected with helminths at first ANC visit and those whose mothers were not infected with any helminth remained significant after adjusting for maternal education, child sex and HOME score (p = 0.013).
294.Rationale: While adjusting for socioeconomic and maternal health factors is important, this does not specifically address anemia as a confounder. The question asks for a factor that most strongly suggests the association is not due to anemia. Adjusting for general confounders is a broad approach but does not directly rule out anemia as a specific confounder. The persistence of the association after these adjustments is suggestive but not as strong as adjusting for a known specific confounder like malaria.

294.Option: The association was strongest for hookworm infection, which is the most common helminth species associated with iron deficiency, suggesting a biologically plausible causal pathway
Evidence: Prenatal hookworm infection was related to lower performance in gross motor tests.
Hookworm infection remained associated with ELC scores.
Hookworms are a known risk factor for ID.
294.Rationale: While a biologically plausible pathway is important, it does not rule out confounding by anemia. The fact that the association is strongest for hookworm, which is associated with iron deficiency, could actually suggest that anemia is a mediator rather than a confounder. A biologically plausible pathway supports the possibility of a causal relationship but does not eliminate the possibility that anemia is confounding the association.

296.Option: The observation that minor variants are primarily found at frequencies below 1%, which is below the detection limit of Sanger sequencing, supports the idea that immune pressure generates rare, transient antigenic variants that are quickly eliminated
Evidence: The frequency of all detected minor variants was almost below 20% (231/237) (Fig 2A). To investigate the exact relative frequency distribution of minor variants, we used three thresholds to explore the characteristics (Fig 2B). The major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range, and the lowest was in the 10–60% (22/237) range.

However, it is an advantage of NGS to fully discover the variants [34, 35]. Combined with the use of an in-house Perl script, we were able to retrieve the variants within the regions of each strain and calculate the relative frequency of the variants, thus disclosing the proportion of these variant sequences in primary syphilis patients.
296.Rationale: The passage states that minor variants were found at frequencies between 1-5% (181/237) and not below 1%. The option incorrectly claims they are below 1%, which is below Sanger's detection limit. The correct evidence shows they are detectable by NGS but not Sanger, but the option's claim about frequencies is factually incorrect based on the passage.

296.Option: The high frequency of a single predominant sequence in most V regions, especially V1, indicates that immune pressure stabilizes a conserved antigenic profile early in infection
Evidence: As Fig 2A shows, there was a predominant sequence in each V region of ten samples directly from primary syphilis patients, and the proportion of this sequence was almost above 80%.
The findings could provide important information for further exploration of the role of tprK in immune evasion and persistent infection with syphilis.
296.Rationale: While the passage notes a high frequency of predominant sequences, it explicitly links this to immune evasion strategies rather than stabilization of a conserved profile. The high frequency is interpreted as a fitness-based adaptation to avoid immune recognition, not as evidence of immune pressure stabilizing a profile.

296.Option: The increase in frequency of minor variants in V6, a region with the highest genetic diversity, during early infection suggests immune-driven selection for antigenic escape
Evidence: Among the seven V regions, V1 and V6 were found to have the lowest and highest variability, respectively (Figs 1 and 2A), which was consistent with the findings of previous studies [24, 33].

Additionally, among these four V regions, the frequency of the predominant sequence in V6 was particularly low (Fig 2A), suggesting that V6 may be the first affected region and is involved in immune evasion during the course of infection [21, 24].
296.Rationale: The passage directly states that V6 has the highest variability and that its low predominant sequence frequency suggests it is the first region affected by immune evasion. This directly supports the hypothesis that immune pressure drives antigenic diversity in V6 during early infection.

296.Option: The presence of multiple length variants in V3, V6, and V7, which are the most variable regions, indicates that structural changes in TprK are driven by immune pressure to evade host recognition
Evidence: The size range of the captured sequences was the largest for V3, V6 and V7, which was similar to the findings of Pinto et al. [24], demonstrating that the variations in these three regions could more easily cause changes in length.
A change pattern characterized multiple of 3 bp matched the triplet codon in protein coding, which has made us think that this feature probably explains why it is rare to uncover a tprK frame shift.
296.Rationale: While the passage notes length variability in these regions, it attributes this to a strict gene conversion mechanism maintaining codon alignment rather than direct immune pressure. The structural changes are framed as a byproduct of genetic mechanisms, not as a direct immune evasion strategy.

296.Option: The high inter-population redundancy of amino acid sequences in V1 and V4, despite overall tprK variability, suggests that immune pressure is strongest in these regions, leading to conserved antigenic profiles across patients
Evidence: The 14 strains are specified by coloured solid circles, and the predominant sequence and minor variants within each V region of one strain are represented by a bold arc and thin arcs, respectively. Each grey circle indicates the occurrence of sequence consistency among the strains.

The similar findings that were observed between the two studies using different approaches to investigate the adaptive traits of the pathogen during different human infection were exciting and clearly suggest the existence of better fitted antigenic profiles to address the immune response of the host.
296.Rationale: The passage explains the inter-population redundancy in V1 and V4 as evidence of 'better fitted antigenic profiles' rather than strongest immune pressure. The conservation is interpreted as adaptive success, not as a result of strongest immune pressure.

297.Option: Implement population-wide door-to-door screening every six months to detect all cases of PKDL and leprosy in the region
Evidence: The teams were able to screen 25,686 individuals, i.e., 77.1% of the population. With a few exceptions, those not screened were not present at the time of the visit.
Men were underrepresented, making up only 41.7% of the population screened; in the age group above 20 years, only 30.7% were men.
A limitation of our current survey is the fact that young adult men are under represented.
297.Rationale: Population-wide door-to-door screening every six months is resource-intensive and logistically challenging, as demonstrated by the study's 77.1% screening rate and underrepresentation of certain groups like men. The study also notes that such an approach is 'probably the most effective way to find the few hidden PKDL cases but unfortunately will never be sustainable' due to the time and resources required.

297.Option: Prioritize screening for leprosy in households with a history of VL to detect potential PKDL cases due to shared risk factors
Evidence: The three PKDL cases were all from different villages.
Given their small numbers, a further spatial analysis was not considered useful.
297.Rationale: This option incorrectly assumes a direct link between leprosy and PKDL. The study found that PKDL cases were not spatially clustered and were from different villages, indicating that shared risk factors with leprosy are not a reliable basis for screening. Additionally, leprosy and PKDL are distinct diseases with different transmission dynamics and risk factors.

297.Option: Use passive case finding through health facility reporting to identify new cases of PKDL and leprosy in areas with high leprosy prevalence
Evidence: Responding to the basic health care needs of the population and seizing the opportunity to also search for PKDL and leprosy at the same time could be the most efficient way forward.
The skin camps in our study were very well attended. Most attendees, however, do not attend with complaints caused by PKDL or leprosy but because of other much more prevalent skin conditions.
297.Rationale: Passive case finding through health facility reporting is likely to miss many cases, especially PKDL, which may be asymptomatic or underreported. The study suggests that opportunistic screening at skin camps is more effective, as passive methods alone may not capture the hidden transmission risks.

297.Option: Conduct targeted active case finding around known leprosy cases and follow up past VL patients for PKDL to detect hidden transmission risks
Evidence: The increased risk for those living within 25 meters of a leprosy affected household found in our study confirms the observations of Moura et al. from Brazil who found equally high prevalence rates in households of neighbors of leprosy patients as in the patient households.
For PKDL this should be combined with active follow-up of previously treated VL cases.

A combination of targeted active case finding for leprosy, with screening for PKDL in hotspots of previous transmission, and regular skin camps seems the most realistic approach to tackle both diseases.
297.Rationale: This option aligns with the study's findings that leprosy cases are spatially clustered and that PKDL can be detected through follow-up of past VL patients. Targeted active case finding around known leprosy cases and follow-up of past VL patients is a cost-effective and sustainable approach that addresses the spatial patterns of both diseases while minimizing resource use.

297.Option: Establish skin camps in villages with the highest historical VL incidence to detect PKDL and leprosy cases through opportunistic screening
Evidence: The skin camps in our study were very well attended. Most attendees, however, do not attend with complaints caused by PKDL or leprosy but because of other much more prevalent skin conditions.
Responding to the basic health care needs of the population and seizing the opportunity to also search for PKDL and leprosy at the same time could be the most efficient way forward.

A combination of targeted active case finding for leprosy, with screening for PKDL in hotspots of previous transmission, and regular skin camps seems the most realistic approach to tackle both diseases.
297.Rationale: Establishing skin camps in villages with high historical VL incidence leverages opportunistic screening to detect PKDL and leprosy cases. This approach is efficient and well-attended, as demonstrated by the study, and allows for addressing both common and rare skin conditions simultaneously, making it a practical and sustainable strategy.

298.Option: The route of administration of the monoclonal antibody, such as intraperitoneal versus intravenous delivery
Evidence: The MAbs were given i.p. at 1 dpi with 1,000 LD50 of GA-ZEBOV
All MAbs were once again tested individually in the guinea pig model. The MAbs were given i.p. at 1 dpi with 1,000 LD50 of GA-ZEBOV
The MAbs were added to an equal volume of VSVΔG/ZEBOVGP for 1 hour at 37°C before adding to a confluent 12 well tissue culture plate seeded with Vero E6 cells
298.Rationale: The passage consistently uses intraperitoneal (i.p.) administration in both mouse and guinea pig models, but does not compare different routes of administration (e.g., intravenous) or suggest that route affects protection outcomes. The study focuses on timing, dose, and epitope specificity rather than route of administration as a critical factor for protection.

298.Option: The presence of a neutralizing antibody response in the host's endogenous immune system at the time of treatment
Evidence: The MAbs were generated using the VSVΔG/ZEBOVGP vaccine as the immunogen
The PRNT50 demonstrated that MAbs 1H3, 2G4, and 4G7 were neutralizing with a PRNT50 at a 1/200, 1/800, and 1/6,400 dilution, respectively
In fatally infected patients EBOV-specific IgG was absent, and IgM levels were low in comparison to the survivors
298.Rationale: The study evaluates exogenous monoclonal antibodies (MAbs) as passive immunotherapy, not the host's endogenous immune response. The passage discusses the effectiveness of MAbs in the absence of host neutralizing antibodies, particularly in the guinea pig model where only 2G4 and 4G7 provided 60% survival. The focus is on the administered MAbs' properties, not the host's pre-existing immune response.

298.Option: The total dose of monoclonal antibodies administered, regardless of timing relative to infection
Evidence: A dose response was observed, and some MAbs were more potent than others for a given dose
The lowest doses providing complete protection from lethality for 5D2, 5E6, 7C9, and 7G4 were 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 µg, respectively
MAbs 4G7 and 10C8 demonstrated an 83% survival rate at the highest dose of 100 µg
298.Rationale: While the study shows a dose-dependent protective effect, the passage emphasizes that timing relative to infection is a critical factor. For example, even at the highest dose (100 µg), some MAbs failed to provide complete protection when treatment was delayed beyond 2 days post-infection. The effectiveness of the MAbs is not solely determined by dose but also by the timing of administration.

298.Option: The specific epitope targeted by the monoclonal antibody on the Ebola virus glycoprotein
Evidence: 1H3 and 4G7 bind to the N- and C-terminus of GP1, respectively, whereas 2G4 binds to GP2
Each of these MAbs binds to different regions of GP1,2

There have been several attempts at producing MAbs against ZEBOV GP however no clear pattern has emerged suggesting which primary sequence domain of GP is most immunogenic or whether neutralizing antibodies are more successful
298.Rationale: The study acknowledges that different MAbs target various epitopes on the glycoprotein (GP1 and GP2), but it does not identify a single epitope as the most critical for complete protection. The passage states that no clear pattern has emerged regarding which epitope is most effective, suggesting that epitope specificity alone is not the strongest determinant of protection when treatment is delayed.

298.Option: The specific timing of treatment relative to infection onset, particularly whether it occurs within 2 days post-infection, is the most critical factor for achieving complete protection
Evidence: In general, the MAbs worked best for both animal models when given after the start of the infection, particularly at 1 and 2 dpi, before efficacy started to decrease at 3 dpi
This suggests that there may be a limited time period in which to begin treatment after becoming infected
The treatment could be delivered as late as 2 days after infection
Delaying the treatment to 3 dpi resulted in no survival for all MAbs except for 4G7 (10% survival rate)
The 2 dpi treatment protocol being the most effective
298.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that the timing of treatment relative to infection is the most critical factor for achieving complete protection. It explicitly states that MAbs are most effective when administered within 2 days post-infection (dpi), with efficacy declining significantly at 3 dpi. The study also notes that a combination of MAbs could be delivered as late as 2 days after infection to achieve complete protection, highlighting the importance of timing over other factors like dose or epitope specificity.

299.Option: Select the cut-off value that corresponds to the lowest antibody level observed in any confirmed VL case to ensure no false negatives
Evidence: The test showed positivity in 95 of 97 VL cases, yielding a sensitivity of 97.94% for detection of Leishmania exposure. False negative results were found for 2 (2.06%) of 97 VL samples.
All 14 PKDL samples showed antibody titres above the cut-off line thus 100% sensitive for urine ELISA.
Overall specificity was calculated using 75 non-VL urine samples of controls and found 100% specificity without any false positives.
299.Rationale: Selecting the lowest antibody level in confirmed cases would maximize sensitivity but likely reduce specificity. The passage shows that 2.06% of VL cases were false negatives, indicating that some infected individuals have lower antibody levels. Using the lowest observed value would include all these cases but would likely include many uninfected individuals as false positives, which is not ideal for a diagnostic test.

299.Option: Use a receiver-operating characteristic (ROC) curve to identify the optimal cut-off point that maximizes both sensitivity and specificity
Evidence: To assess the overall performance of the test sensitivity and specificity were calculated and diagnostic accuracy was established by area under the curve (AUC) where AUC = 1 indicates an accurate test.

ROC curve obtained from the ELISA values for the detection of anti-Leishmania IgG antibodies in urine samples. Cut-off value (0.1875), sensitivity (97.94%), specificity (100%) and AUC (0.9984) were determined by this curve using GraphPad Prism software (version 5.0).
299.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes the use of a ROC curve to determine the optimal cut-off value (0.1875) that balances sensitivity (97.94%) and specificity (100%). This is the standard epidemiological method for determining diagnostic thresholds as it optimizes the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity.

299.Option: Set the cut-off value at the mean antibody level of the control group plus two standard deviations to minimize false positives
Evidence: Overall specificity was calculated using 75 non-VL urine samples of controls and found 100% specificity without any false positives.
The test showed positivity in 95 of 97 VL cases, yielding a sensitivity of 97.94% for detection of Leishmania exposure.
299.Rationale: While this method focuses on minimizing false positives, it ignores the distribution of antibody levels in the infected population. The passage shows that the test achieved 100% specificity without any false positives, but this was achieved through a ROC-derived cut-off rather than a statistical method based solely on the control group. This approach would likely miss some infected individuals with lower antibody levels, reducing sensitivity.

299.Option: Use the median antibody level of the VL-positive group as the cut-off to ensure equal distribution of values above and below the threshold
Evidence: The test showed positivity in 95 of 97 VL cases, yielding a sensitivity of 97.94% for detection of Leishmania exposure.
All 14 PKDL samples showed antibody titres above the cut-off line thus 100% sensitive for urine ELISA.
299.Rationale: Using the median of the infected group as a cut-off would maximize sensitivity but likely reduce specificity. The passage shows that the test achieved high sensitivity (97.94%) and perfect specificity (100%) through a ROC-derived cut-off, not by using the median of the infected group. This approach would likely include many uninfected individuals as false positives.

300.Option: Respondents may have misreported their feeding behavior due to social desirability bias, especially among those with negative attitudes toward free-roaming dogs
Evidence: Similarly, the respondents who fed them surprisingly had a lower acceptance for FRD (OR 0.08).

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
300.Rationale: The passage indicates that feeding stray dogs is influenced by religious convictions rather than social desirability bias. The lower acceptance for FRD among those who feed them suggests a genuine discrepancy rather than misreporting. The text explicitly attributes feeding behavior to religious beliefs, not social desirability bias.

300.Option: The inconsistency between feeding and attitudes is due to a lack of understanding about the transmission of rabies through licks and scratches, which leads to underestimation of risk
Evidence: Although the majority of the respondents were aware that rabies: could be transmitted through dog-bites, is fatal once clinical signs develop and can be prevented through post-bite anti-rabies vaccination or prophylactic vaccination of dogs, most were unaware it could be transmitted through licks/scratches from a rabid animal or through rabid cats.

Unexpectedly more people with a good overall understanding of the practices to adopt to control rabies did not consider wound cleaning beneficial (OR 3.6, p = 0.009) as reported in other studies also undertaken in India [38–40].
300.Rationale: The passage states that most respondents were unaware of transmission through licks/scratches, but this is not the primary factor explaining the discrepancy in feeding practices. The inconsistency is more directly linked to religious beliefs and perceived responsibility for feeding, not transmission knowledge. The text emphasizes religious motivations over transmission understanding as the key factor.

300.Option: Religious beliefs promoting animal care override risk awareness, leading to feeding despite negative attitudes toward free-roaming dogs
Evidence: It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
Similarly, the respondents who fed them surprisingly had a lower acceptance for FRD (OR 0.08).
300.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes feeding behavior to religious convictions, particularly Hinduism's tolerance of animals. This explains the discrepancy between feeding practices and negative attitudes toward FRD. The text directly states that religious beliefs drive feeding despite negative perceptions, making this the most accurate explanation.

300.Option: Higher socio-economic status is associated with greater access to information about rabies, leading to more consistent reporting of feeding practices and attitudes
Evidence: Respondents from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005), and would take an injured stray dog to a veterinarian (OR 2.33 95%CI 1.0–5.48, p = 0.04).
Respondents from a low socio-economic level were less likely to consider FRD useful, feed them or take an injured dog to a veterinarian than participants from high/middle levels.
300.Rationale: The passage indicates that higher socio-economic status is associated with more positive attitudes toward FRD and feeding, but this does not explain the discrepancy between feeding and negative attitudes. The text emphasizes religious beliefs as the primary driver of feeding behavior, not socio-economic status.

300.Option: The discrepancy arises because respondents from lower socio-economic groups are more likely to feed dogs out of necessity, while higher SES groups feed them out of compassion, leading to divergent attitudes
Evidence: Respondents from a low socio-economic level were less likely to consider FRD useful, feed them or take an injured dog to a veterinarian than participants from high/middle levels.

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
300.Rationale: The passage attributes feeding behavior to religious beliefs rather than necessity or compassion based on socio-economic status. The text explicitly states that religious convictions drive feeding, not socio-economic motivations. Lower socio-economic groups are less likely to feed dogs, contradicting the option's claim.

301.Option: The pathogen is transmitted directly from infected fleas to their offspring and persists across life stages without requiring vertebrate hosts
Evidence: "It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4]."
"It is unknown if vertebrate reservoirs contribute to its transmission cycle; the bacterium is transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas."
301.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that R. felis is 'naturally passaged generationally in fleas' and 'transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas.' This directly supports the hypothesis that the pathogen is transmitted from infected fleas to their offspring and persists across life stages without requiring vertebrate hosts. This is a strong indicator of a transmission cycle that does not depend on vertebrate reservoirs, as the pathogen can be maintained and replicated within the arthropod vector itself.

301.Option: The pathogen causes severe neurological disease in humans, indicating a high pathogenic potential that likely requires sustained circulation in vertebrate populations
Evidence: "In this report we describe the identification of R. felis from the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of two fatal cases of acute neurological disease in North Sulawesi, Indonesia."
301.Rationale: The fact that R. felis causes severe neurological disease in humans does not necessarily indicate that it requires sustained circulation in vertebrate populations. In fact, the passage notes that the role of vertebrate reservoirs in the transmission cycle of R. felis is still unclear. The severity of the disease in humans does not provide evidence for or against the need for vertebrate reservoirs in the transmission cycle.

301.Option: The pathogen was first isolated from cat fleas, indicating that fleas are the primary and exclusive vector, which supports a transmission cycle independent of vertebrate hosts
Evidence: "Rickettsia felis was first identified as a R. typhi-like organism in the midgut epithelium of a commercial colony of cat fleas, Ctenocephalides felis [1]."

"It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4]."
301.Rationale: While the pathogen was first isolated from cat fleas, the passage does not state that fleas are the 'primary and exclusive vector.' In fact, it mentions that R. felis has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, and that the role of vertebrate reservoirs is still unclear. Therefore, the isolation of the pathogen from cat fleas does not definitively support a transmission cycle independent of vertebrate hosts.

301.Option: The pathogen is not detected in human blood samples, suggesting it is not adapted to vertebrate hosts and thus likely maintained solely in arthropods
Evidence: "Because Rickettsia invade endothelial cells, DNA is typically scarce in sera or CSF [23]."
301.Rationale: The passage states that Rickettsia DNA is typically scarce in sera or CSF, not that it is not detected at all. The absence of detection in blood samples could be due to the pathogen's tendency to invade endothelial cells, making it less detectable in the bloodstream. This does not necessarily mean the pathogen is not adapted to vertebrate hosts or is maintained solely in arthropods.

301.Option: The pathogen has been detected in multiple arthropod species, including mosquitoes and fleas, suggesting a broad vector range that supports transmission without vertebrate hosts
Evidence: "It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4]."
301.Rationale: The detection of the pathogen in multiple arthropod species suggests a broad vector range, but it does not necessarily support transmission without vertebrate hosts. The passage explicitly states that the role of vertebrate reservoirs in the transmission cycle is still unclear. Therefore, the presence of the pathogen in multiple arthropod species does not provide strong evidence for a transmission cycle independent of vertebrate hosts.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate of 41.3% in elderly dengue patients justifies the use of any available laboratory test for early risk stratification
Evidence: The in-hospital CFR was 41.3% (31 patients), and these patients had a median ICU stay of 4 (IQR: 1–8) days.

302.Rationale: While the high CFR emphasizes the need for risk stratification, the statement that 'any available laboratory test' should be used is not supported by the passage. The passage specifically identifies APTT as a valuable single test, not any test. The high mortality rate is a context for the study but does not justify the use of any test for stratification.

302.Option: Prolonged APTT at ICU admission remains an independent predictor of in-hospital fatality even after accounting for multi-parameter severity scores
Evidence: In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s (Table 3). APACHE II and SOFA scores include many clinical parameters and were not included in the final model of multivariate Cox analysis as they demonstrated collinearity with other variables. They were positively associated with acute respiratory failure, AKI, and cardiac arrest. Creatinine was also not included in the final model due to collinearity with the presence of AKI at ICU admission.

Moreover, APTT prolongation > 48 s remained to be an independent predictive factor for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for APACHE II score (HR: 3.34, P = 0.006), and was marginally significant after adjusting for SOFA score (HR: 2.43, P = 0.053).
302.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that APTT prolongation remains an independent predictor of in-hospital fatality even after adjusting for multi-parameter severity scores like APACHE II and SOFA. This directly supports the use of APTT as a single laboratory test for early risk stratification despite the availability of more complex scoring systems.

302.Option: High APACHE II and SOFA scores are strongly associated with in-hospital fatality and therefore should be prioritized for early risk stratification in elderly dengue patients
Evidence: APACHE II and SOFA scores include many clinical parameters and were not included in the final model of multivariate Cox analysis as they demonstrated collinearity with other variables. They were positively associated with acute respiratory failure, AKI, and cardiac arrest.

The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94). Though there was no significant difference between APTT and two scores (APTT vs. APACHE II, P = 0.27; APTT vs. SOFA, P = 0.16), AUCs of APACHE II and SOFA scores, which both were a constellation of multiple clinical and laboratory variables, were higher than that of APTT. However, APTT, a single laboratory parameter, exhibited valuable prognostic significance.
302.Rationale: While APACHE II and SOFA scores are associated with fatality, the passage explicitly states that they were excluded from the final model due to collinearity with other variables. Additionally, the study emphasizes the value of APTT as a single test despite the higher AUCs of the multi-parameter scores, suggesting that APTT is a more practical and independent predictor for early stratification.

302.Option: APTT prolongation is more sensitive than APACHE II and SOFA scores for detecting early signs of organ failure in elderly dengue patients
Evidence: The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94). Though there was no significant difference between APTT and two scores (APTT vs. APACHE II, P = 0.27; APTT vs. SOFA, P = 0.16), AUCs of APACHE II and SOFA scores, which both were a constellation of multiple clinical and laboratory variables, were higher than that of APTT. However, APTT, a single laboratory parameter, exhibited valuable prognostic significance.

APTT prolongation > 48 s remained to be an independent predictive factor for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for APACHE II score (HR: 3.34, P = 0.006), and was marginally significant after adjusting for SOFA score (HR: 2.43, P = 0.053).
302.Rationale: The passage does not state that APTT is more sensitive than APACHE II or SOFA scores. In fact, the AUCs for APACHE II and SOFA scores are higher than that of APTT. The passage emphasizes APTT's value as a single test despite its lower AUC, not because it is more sensitive.

302.Option: The presence of acute respiratory failure at ICU admission is a stronger predictor of fatality than APTT prolongation and should be used for early risk stratification
Evidence: In the univariate Cox model, female gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, high creatinine level, AKI at ICU admission, a high APACHE II or SOFA score, and APTT longer than 48 s were significantly associated with in-hospital fatality. In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s (Table 3).

In the Cox model, although respiratory failure indicative of a deterioration of clinical status and dysregulation in respiratory function, at ICU admission, was marginally significant, respiratory failure still remains to be an important prognostic factor.
302.Rationale: While respiratory failure is an important prognostic factor, the multivariate analysis shows that APTT prolongation has a higher hazard ratio (3.91) compared to respiratory failure, which was only marginally significant. The passage does not support the claim that respiratory failure is a stronger predictor than APTT prolongation.

304.Option: Use Bayesian inference with a fixed geographic origin for each node to estimate the timing of transmission events

Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools

306.Option: The use of a quantitative ELISA assay to measure circulating filarial antigen levels, enabling detection of changes in adult worm burden over time
Evidence: The TropBio ELISA is measuring the circulating filarial antigen of the adult filarial worm. Values above OD 0.35 are considered as positive, with low positive results between OD 0.35 and 0.8, intermediate positive results between OD 0.8 and 1.0, and high positive results above the OD of 1.0. Most filarial infected individuals in 2009 showed high positive results, reflecting a high adult worm burden. In 2019 infected individuals had low positive ODs indicating a low number of adult worms.

The mean OD values by age group declined from 0.55, 0.63, 0.74 to 0.34, 0.44, 0.44 (38%, 30%, 40.5%) between 2009 and 2010/11, then to 0.08, 0.10, 0.11 (85.4%, 84.1%, 85.1%) in 2019 in participants within the age group range of 14 to <25, 25 to <45 and 45 to < = 66 years respectively (Fig 4A). The mean OD of CFA positive individuals was 1.55 (SD 0.70) in 2009, 1.11 (SD 0.56), in 2010/11 and 0.49 (SD 0.10) in 2019. Of the 22 individuals with positive CFA results in 2019, 18 had high antigen levels during the EMINI 2009 and 2010/11 surveys and there were 4 new infections of previously uninfected participants (Fig 4B).
306.Rationale: While the quantitative ELISA provides more detailed information about the worm burden, it does not directly address the causal inference needed to attribute the reduction in infection to the treatment campaign. The ELISA measures the antigen levels but does not establish a comparison group or control for other potential factors that could have influenced the observed changes.

306.Option: The inclusion of a control group from a non-treatment region to compare changes in filarial prevalence over time
Evidence: The study participants were again visited in 2019, after seven years of annual treatment and additional three years of surveillance. Our results show a dramatic reduction in LF prevalence from 35.1% in 2009, to 1.7% in 2019 among the individuals aged 14 to 65 years.

306.Rationale: The inclusion of a control group from a non-treatment region would provide a direct comparison to assess whether the observed reduction in filarial infection is specific to the treatment campaign in Kyela district. This would help to rule out other factors such as natural fluctuations in infection rates or changes in other environmental or social conditions that could have contributed to the reduction. The absence of a control group limits the ability to attribute the observed changes solely to the treatment campaign.

306.Option: The use of a fixed cohort with repeated measurements over time, which controls for both sampling bias and age-related confounding
Evidence: All households in Kyela who previously participated in the EMINI study ([13]) were contacted to enrol inhabitants aged 14 to 65 years into the RHINO study. Of 2187 potentially eligible individuals with previous data for LF, 1299 (60%) were enrolled into the follow-up activity and 856 (40%) were not found or chose not to participate.

A comparison of the data from the EMINI study shows that age, sex and LF prevalence of individuals who participated in the follow-up activity were similar to those who did not (Table 1). Of note, the LF-prevalence is age-dependent and the study participants were ~ 10 years younger during the EMINI survey.

The study participants were again visited in 2019, after seven years of annual treatment and additional three years of surveillance. Our results show a dramatic reduction in LF prevalence from 35.1% in 2009, to 1.7% in 2019 among the individuals aged 14 to 65 years.
306.Rationale: While the use of a fixed cohort with repeated measurements helps to control for some forms of bias and confounding, it does not provide a comparison to a non-treated group. The study's design does not include a control group, which is necessary to establish a causal relationship between the treatment campaign and the observed reduction in infection. The fixed cohort approach can show trends over time but cannot isolate the effect of the treatment from other temporal changes.

306.Option: The statistical adjustment for age, sex, and occupation in multivariable regression models to control for potential confounders
Evidence: Multi-variable analysis confirmed that W. bancrofti infection was associated with increasing age, male sex and filarial infection during the previous study (Table 3). Major risk factors for the infection with W. bancrofti were male sex, older age, and occupation as a farmer.
306.Rationale: Statistical adjustment for age, sex, and occupation helps to control for potential confounders, but it does not provide a direct comparison to a non-treated group. While this adjustment can strengthen the internal validity of the study, it does not address the need for a control group to establish the causal effect of the treatment campaign. The absence of a control group remains a critical limitation in attributing the observed reduction in infection to the treatment campaign.

307.Option: The significant association between male sex and leptospiral IgM positivity, especially in the 20–29 age group, suggests occupational exposure to infected individuals, supporting direct transmission in high-risk professions

The classical risks factors such as male sex, living in an urban area, and age group were associated with positivity to IgM against Leptospira in multivariable analysis. These findings are consistent with studies identifying Leptospira risk factors[9, 27, 40].
307.Rationale: While the association with male sex and the 20-29 age group is consistent with occupational exposure, the passage explicitly attributes this to environmental risk factors (flood water, building work) rather than direct human-to-human transmission. The option incorrectly interprets occupational exposure as evidence of direct transmission between infected individuals.

307.Option: The seasonal peak of cases during the rainy season, particularly in urban areas, indicates that transmission is driven by increased human mobility and social interaction, supporting a hypothesis of person-to-person spread
Evidence: The seasonality of leptospirosis has been described in most parts of the world, with a significantly higher incidence during rainy months, in tropical areas [38, 39]. We found that cases occur year-round in the DRC, with a higher proportion during the rainy season.

Kinshasa is facing rapid urbanization with an overcrowding population and is prone to flooding after heavy rains. It is described that rapid urbanization, overcrowding, and natural disasters are risk factors promoting the transmission of leptospirosis [36, 37].
307.Rationale: The passage attributes the rainy season peak to environmental factors (flooding, contaminated water) rather than increased human-to-human contact. The option misinterprets the seasonal pattern as evidence of person-to-person transmission when the passage clearly links it to environmental exposure pathways.

307.Option: The high seroprevalence of Leptospira IgM in urban areas, particularly in Kinshasa, suggests that the disease is spreading through dense human populations, indicating possible person-to-person transmission
Evidence: 59% of positive cases lived in an urban setting, and 41% lived in a rural setting. This pattern is consistent with the literature that reports that leptospirosis occurs in both rural and urban settings in tropical and sub-tropical countries [27]. Moreover, our data concur with the literature that suggested that leptospirosis could spread from its traditional environment in rural areas to reach urban areas with poor sanitation infrastructure [28–31].

Leptospirosis is globally known as an urban health problem and the role of urban areas in the re-emergence of leptospirosis has been described by several authors [31–33]. The high seroprevalence in urban settings may relate to poor urbanization (increased risk of flooding), overcrowded populations, as well as knowledge, attitude, and practice determinants.
307.Rationale: While the passage notes high urban prevalence, it explicitly attributes this to environmental factors (flooding, poor sanitation) rather than direct human-to-human transmission. The option incorrectly infers person-to-person spread from urban density without considering the environmental transmission pathway described in the passage.

307.Option: The presence of Leptospira IgM in 14 provinces across the DRC, including both urban and rural areas, indicates widespread human-to-human transmission across diverse populations and regions
Evidence: The geographical distribution of leptospiral IgM seropositivity showed that 25 out of the 26 of the DRC provinces were included in this study and our study population was representative of the country. The option misinterprets geographic spread as evidence of person-to-person transmission without considering the environmental exposure pathway described in the passage.

Evidence: The majority of these Leptospira IgM cases were among young adult males (20–29 years) living in an urban setting. Kinshasa is facing rapid urbanization with an overcrowding population and is prone to flooding after heavy rains. It is described that rapid urbanization, overcrowding, and natural disasters are risk factors promoting the transmission of leptospirosis [36, 37].

The seasonality of leptospirosis has been described in most parts of the world, with a significantly higher incidence during rainy months, in tropical areas [38, 39]. We found that cases occur year-round in the DRC, with a higher proportion during the rainy season.

The classical risks factors such as male sex, living in an urban area, and age group were associated with positivity to IgM against Leptospira in multivariable analysis. These findings are consistent with studies identifying Leptospira risk factors[9, 27, 40]. The significant associations with male sex are consistent with the fact that most occupations at high risk (farmers, miners, recreational activities in muddy grounds, exposures to flood water, building workers) for leptospirosis are often associated with males.
307.Rationale: This option correctly identifies the environmental factors (flooding, poor sanitation) and occupational risks (flood water exposure, building work) as the primary drivers of leptospirosis transmission. 308.Option: The combination of waning insecticidal efficacy and reduced use of complementary protective behaviors likely led to increased dengue exposure despite high curtain usage
Evidence: The results indicate that participants living in the intervention arm were not better protected from DENV exposure than those in the control arm, despite the widespread use of ITCs.

Three factors, however, could have contributed to this unexpected result. First, despite being fabricated from a material that was expected to retain high insecticide levels over the course of several years, the ITCs quickly lost insecticidal efficacy, leading to the need for mass re-treatment only 1-year after they had been originally deployed.

Second, a contributing factor may have been a false sense of security amongst the participants in the intervention arm; i.e., perhaps the presence of the highly visible ITCs created a belief in their protective power among the treated households, who subsequently did not employ any additional measures typically used to avoid exposure to mosquito bites.

Participants that had received ITCs commented that when the curtains were first hung, the household reduced their use of mosquito repellents and stopped fumigating because they felt that it was no longer necessary due to the presence of the ITCs.
308.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the ITCs lost insecticidal efficacy quickly, requiring re-treatment after only 1 year. It also describes a behavioral change where participants in the intervention arm reduced their use of other protective measures due to a false sense of security from the ITCs. This combination of waning efficacy and reduced complementary behaviors likely led to increased dengue exposure despite high curtain usage.

308.Option: The initial entomological improvements observed in the intervention arm were not sustained due to the failure of insecticide-treated curtains to maintain efficacy over time, leading to increased dengue transmission
Evidence: The results indicate that participants living in the intervention arm were not better protected from DENV exposure than those in the control arm, despite the widespread use of ITCs.

Cone bioassays after re-treatment with KO-Tab 123 showed that the curtains did not immediately recover to the 100% bioefficacy observed at baseline.

308.Rationale: While the passage does mention that entomological improvements were not sustained, it also states that the differences were not statistically significant. The primary issue appears to be the combination of waning efficacy and behavioral changes, not just the failure to maintain entomological improvements. The option is incorrect because it oversimplifies the issue and does not account for the behavioral factors mentioned in the passage.

308.Option: The emergence of deltamethrin resistance in the local mosquito population likely reduced the effectiveness of insecticide-treated curtains, leading to increased dengue transmission despite high usage
Evidence: Bioassays detected an increase in resistance to deltamethrin in the local Ae. aegypti population over the course of the study. Resistance was first detected in early 2011, after ITCs had been deployed for over a year, soon after they were re-treated with deltamethrin.

This initial detection of resistance was worrying, because it could indicate that low concentrations of insecticide on ITCs that were not adequately loaded with deltamethrin (the reason why the re-treatment was carried out) had already begun to select for deltamethrin resistance in the local mosquito population.
308.Rationale: While the passage does mention the emergence of deltamethrin resistance, it also states that this resistance was detected after the ITCs had been deployed for over a year and after they were re-treated. The option is incorrect because it oversimplifies the issue and does not account for the other factors mentioned in the passage.

309.Option: The similarity of final clinical response rates between Bangladesh and India supports the generalizability of the results, suggesting that the 6-month clinical endpoint is a valid proxy for parasitological cure in both settings
Evidence: The final clinical response rate (94.2%) is similar to the final clinical response rate observed in the Phase III (94.2% final response) and Phase IV (94.6% final response) studies of PMIM in India [4,5].

The evaluation based on clinical outcome at 6 months was planned for several reasons. This study was conducted with limited resources and was designed similar to a phase 4 study to generate evidence that PMIM works in Bangladesh similar to India, where the etiological agent and patient populations are thought to be very similar. The 6-month observation after treatment was the only practically feasible way to assess a cure in this study because splenic aspiration or other invasive procedures were not appropriate to conduct at the clinical study centers.
309.Rationale: While the similarity of response rates between Bangladesh and India is noted, this does not directly address the critical factor influencing the interpretation of long-term treatment success. The key issue is the validity of using clinical signs as a proxy for parasitological cure, especially in resource-limited settings where parasitological confirmation is not feasible. The 6-month endpoint is a practical choice but may not be a valid proxy for cure without parasitological confirmation.

309.Option: The use of a 6-month follow-up window is sufficient to detect late relapses because the majority of relapses occur within the first 3 months after treatment, minimizing the risk of underestimating true cure rates
Evidence: The evaluation based on clinical outcome at 6 months was planned for several reasons. This study was conducted with limited resources and was designed similar to a phase 4 study to generate evidence that PMIM works in Bangladesh similar to India, where the etiological agent and patient populations are thought to be very similar. The 6-month observation after treatment was the only practically feasible way to assess a cure in this study because splenic aspiration or other invasive procedures were not appropriate to conduct at the clinical study centers.
309.Rationale: The 6-month follow-up window was chosen due to resource limitations and the infeasibility of invasive procedures. However, the assumption that most relapses occur within the first 3 months is not supported by the passage. The study does not provide evidence that the 6-month window is sufficient to detect late relapses, which could lead to an underestimation of true cure rates.

309.Option: The presence of reversible hearing impairment in two patients confirms that ototoxicity is a minor risk and does not significantly affect long-term follow-up or interpretation of treatment success
Evidence: Paromomycin belongs to the aminoglycoside class of agents, which are known to cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. Patients with clinically significant renal dysfunction or history of hearing loss were excluded from this study. Nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity were not formally studied in this trial. During the study, 2 subjects reported adverse events related to hearing loss after end of treatment: moderate ear pruritus in an adult female and moderate hearing impairment and tinnitus in an adult male who also had moderate renal impairment. All of these events were reversible.
309.Rationale: The presence of reversible hearing impairment in two patients does not confirm that ototoxicity is a minor risk. The passage notes that ototoxicity is a known risk of aminoglycosides, and the exclusion of patients with renal dysfunction or hearing loss does not eliminate the risk for all participants. The focus of the question is on the interpretation of long-term treatment success, and ototoxicity is a safety concern rather than a factor affecting the interpretation of cure rates.

309.Option: The reliance on clinical signs rather than parasitological confirmation for assessing cure may underestimate true relapse rates due to limited diagnostic capacity and potential treatment-related loss to follow-up
Evidence: The evaluation based on clinical outcome at 6 months was planned for several reasons. This study was conducted with limited resources and was designed similar to a phase 4 study to generate evidence that PMIM works in Bangladesh similar to India, where the etiological agent and patient populations are thought to be very similar. The 6-month observation after treatment was the only practically feasible way to assess a cure in this study because splenic aspiration or other invasive procedures were not appropriate to conduct at the clinical study centers.
Data on occurrences of PKDL cases after end of treatment were not collected.
309.Rationale: The study explicitly states that parasitological confirmation was not feasible due to resource limitations and the inappropriateness of invasive procedures in the clinical setting. Reliance on clinical signs alone for assessing cure may lead to an underestimation of true relapse rates, as clinical signs can be misleading and may not accurately reflect the presence of parasites. Additionally, the lack of data on PKDL cases further supports the concern that the true relapse rates may be underestimated.

309.Option: The high compliance rate of 98.3% ensures that the final clinical response rate accurately reflects the true effectiveness of paromomycin, minimizing bias from treatment non-adherence
Evidence: A total of 118/120 subjects (98.3%) were compliant with treatment (21 daily injections over no more than 22 days).

The compliance rate of 98.3% in this study is similar to the 98% compliance rate in the Phase IV study conducted in 506 children and adults in an outpatient setting in India [5]. These high compliance rates in rural outpatient treatment settings further supports the use of PMIM in rural endemic areas of Bangladesh.
309.Rationale: While a high compliance rate is beneficial, it does not ensure that the final clinical response rate accurately reflects the true effectiveness of paromomycin. The critical factor influencing the interpretation of long-term treatment success is the validity of the outcome measure (clinical signs vs. parasitological confirmation). Even with high compliance, the use of clinical signs alone may not accurately reflect true cure rates, especially in the absence of parasitological confirmation.

310.Option: Compare the proportion of patients with ECG abnormalities at 5-year intervals post-treatment to assess changes in protection over time
Evidence: A total of 234 patients (110 males, 124 females) with at least 6 years follow-up were evaluated.
During the follow-up period, alterations in routine ECG were observed in 11/234 (4.7%, 95% CI [2–7.4%]).
